speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Felix Pierson, August 12, 1996. You were telling us about the photograph of your father you took out of your pocket. You had noticed his gray beard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think I took it out from his pocket, and I kept it. And he looked normal. He was tanned and all that, so I put it in front of his face. I don't know who the man was that was separating left to right, but I understood that he's looking for people to go to work. So I kept this picture in front of my father's face. I said, Schneider Meister, Schneider Meister, and he took him with the living. And I wore a navy blouse with a zipper that my father made-- he dyed linen, white linen to cover-- I think I mentioned it before. And I don't know where I got the idea. I took a needle with white thread and on the pocket, I wound it around like that and telling him, also Schneider. At that time it was '42. I was 16 years old. I was a Schneider like I'm a rabbi. But anyway, I knew a little bit, of course, I grew up around it and I worked already in the Lask ghetto. So we were taken with, I think about 300 people-- I  don't remember exactly how many-- we were taken out of town, which was really maybe a kilometer away, and put in a big ditch. And around that ditch, there were these guards standing with guns, but it was already dark, so it really-- and we cuddled together and this is how we stay there overnight, in that ditch. In the morning-- I don't remember if it was one night or two nights. God, memory lapses. So we're taken out in groups of 20. 20, 30, it was  very small groups. We were taken up to another-- across the street-- but it was a pile of stones and on that pile of stones, a man stood that introduce himself as [NON-ENGLISH]. Now [NON-ENGLISH] was Himmler's son-in-law, and it seems that they were concerned if we would resist because they tried to-- I wouldn't say appease us-- but keep us calm. He told us in German that we're going to work, being shipped to work, where we will have [GERMAN], apples and oranges and anything that we desire, as long as we will devote ourselves for the good of the Third Reich. At the same time, they asked if anyone has any diamonds or jewelry, you better give it to them now because we will be searched. People, whatever they had hidden-- I don't know if everybody, but things were being given to them. And that was continued for the groups and these were the groups that were being-- these 300 people are being sent to-- I think it was 300. That's what it is in my mind. But from these supposedly healthy, work-capable people, they had picked 20 people that were sent to Chelmno to dig graves for those that remained in the synagogue. Those 20 people never came back. Some people, I remember distinctly, there was [NON-ENGLISH], my friend that lives here in Florida with a brother. There was two brothers, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't remember-- I just remember-- but they dug the grave, they also dug a grave for themselves, where they were later shot and thrown into that grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there we were sent by the railroad. Why can't I remember? I think it was the railroad. Anyway, we wound up in Lodz, in the Lodz ghetto. When we arrived, they picked a group of young people, including me, to clean out-- go back to our town and clean out the houses, put them in bundles and put them on trains. And when I came back, my father had already a room. Was it just men in that group that was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were women, too. Yes. And I became a housekeeper where I took care. From the rations that we got, I cooked and scrubbed the floor, and remembered my mother boiling the wash, but we had nothing in our bone-- nothing. No clothing, nothing. We were given, from time to time, some clothing, but we didn't have and we didn't need. And I remember cooking these meals for my father and one time-- I don't know where I learned to cook but we had-- when I couldn't think of the word before, horseradish are the small one, but these are the big ones. Only this, as much as you wanted. So I used to make rice from that. Then we would get pumpkins. They would cut pumpkins. Well, but that was only one shot in a long time, but I had a little bit more but normally. Then I can particularly remember where I cooked a meal for me and my father. We had five potatoes-- five pieces of potatoes. I cut the potatoes, whatever I had, in five pieces, and I poured it into a bowl. Into my father's bowl, I put three pieces, and in mine, I took two. Respect for-- no matter. I was so hungry. God almighty. No matter how you hear and whatever people tell you, you don't-- unless you experience this, you don't know what hunger is. It drains your [NON-ENGLISH]. You see it. It's there. And yet, I put the three pieces in my father's bowl. And as I turned-- I don't remember, to bring some salt or some-- for whatever reason. I found the third piece in my bowl. I probably never was so angry at my father in all the years that we were together. I wanted it so badly but yet, I knew that I have to preserve my father's life. He was everything. He was our shield. He was our-- it was father. When he laid down at back home or took a rest after dinner-- then we had early dinner, like 2 o'clock-- and customers could come in and a customer was important. No one would wake my father. This is the kind of respect we gave him. And my father, like I mentioned before, had problems with his stomach so he went to these spas. And when we came into the ghetto, my father said, I never felt so good. I now know why. That fat was used up, but after three months that fat was missing, and he started to get those boils on his neck that-- I remember, for the rest of the time that we were together, he had constant boils and it was bandaged and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did the best how I could to take care of my father. I remember, when we used to get a bread for the week-- each person. So my father, he would cut up in the evening this bread into four pieces because it was for-- we had two breads, right? So when we cut up the pieces, my father would say, you see my child? This piece is uneven. Let's even it out. So he would even it out. For me a piece, for him a piece. By the time it remained for the next day to live on, there wasn't much left because we kept evening it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hunger was beyond description, and we lived like that in the ghetto for about-- let's see, from '42 to '44. No pleasures, no relaxation. Constant work and I was happy that I had this kind of work where I worked with the juveniles as assistant foreman because I received an extra half liter of soup. That was life. That was life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the ghetto, Lodz ghetto, a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll describe as much as I know. I didn't go visiting. I just went from where we lived, to work, and on the free day, wherever we had the rations, I would go to pick up the rations or my father would pick it up, but there was no-- people that lived in Lodz, they probably socialized in a way. They visited old friends. There was no-- the only visit I remember, one time we went to see an uncle that-- he was a superintendent, and these people had a little bit more to eat. So we were hoping that we might get something there, but we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, everyone was looking out for themselves. It was just constant work. Every day, we heard of new problems, of new people being deported to different places. We saw the hunger and the poverty of people in the street was-- children was-- there were no children. People were standing and selling artificial [INAUDIBLE] and-- you know what I used to eat? From the peels of the potatoes, we would make pancakes. I remember from [NON-ENGLISH] making hamburgers. We used to call them [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] of is like the artificial coal that you put in a barbecue? What are these called? Charcoal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charcoal. But you put a match to it and it lights automatically. This is how they tasted. But we ate. We ate just to fill up the stomach. And I constantly saw people in the street moving those dead bodies because people kept dying. And the situation wasn't getting better. You heard some rumors about the war, but it was all rumors. And in all truth, I didn't want to hear anything. My father knew these things. All I did is went to work, I came home from work. I knew I had to prepare that meal, then take care on laundry. But you had to wash. And I didn't just wash. I remember my mother, seeing boiling stuff and I did that. And I finished the work, and I went to sleep. That was the life in ghetto. I know that people that were born in Lodz, there were some other interests that they had, but this was my experience. It was two years that, as time goes by, certain things are being-- they disappear from your memory, but every day was survival. Fight for survival. I worked in the factory with young children, and if they sewed a seam that had to be opened, we had to make good that thread. So either we sold a piece of bread-- this is how we were under constant threat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto was-- where we lived we had to go over a bridge across the street and then I walked. It was maybe half hour, 3/4 of an hour walk? People walked in those wooden shoes. That's where we walked, wearing the wooden shoes. I think these were the shoes like the Holland people had, not the kind that my grandfather made because my grandfather made them with leather. This was pure wood and wintertime was even worse. And then, towards the end, I and my father, we tried to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, one time my father went to take out the rations which we have received and Rumkowski, the leader of the ghetto, surrounded that place and checked the IDs of the people that were getting their rations. And he found out that my father should have been at work and not getting the rations, and he put him in the prison where from this prison no one came back alive. No one came back. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], and if you were sent to [NON-ENGLISH] that means you were sent outside the ghetto. Well, here I was this young boy, very little experience in life except these things that were happening during the war years. I ran to the-- in every factory you had the police commissar, and I ran into him. He knew my father, and he knew-- first I went to the technical leader, [GERMAN], and he took me to the commissioner and talked about my father. He was a good worker, et cetera, et cetera. He got him out. Or he came back, you know. At that time, I remember, whatever potatoes I had in the house, I had boiled them and brought them to the prison for my father. I figured, that's his last night, at least don't let him go hungry. When he came back we had nothing to eat, but it was worth it. We helped each other. If not for my father, I wouldn't be sitting here today. My father saved my life. Later on, he saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the order came to leave the ghetto, which was in August '44, we tried to hide. And we were so lucky to pick a hiding place where underneath was a bakery, and that smell of the bread was killing us. We had no food. So my father went out, and he organized some leafs from beets where we were able to eat that for a day, but finally, we decided to give up. And everyone received a bread at the deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we were going. So I remember getting this bread and eating 3/4 of it and now that I wasn't hungry anymore, I took that last quarter. And I put it on a shelf on the wagon that we were going to Birkenau. When I woke up, of course, it wasn't there. And we were told that we're going to work. We didn't realize that we're going to a place where there is so much death. And when we finally arrived at night-- I think it was at night-- in Birkenau, didn't know where we were. All we heard is people screaming, [GERMAN], and chased with dogs, and somebody separated people left and the right. And again, I wound up with my father in one of those huts where we stayed for eight days. There was something going on in that Birkenau. People were so afraid. Everyone was whispering to the next one, bad things are going on there. I didn't want to know from anything because I just wanted to be with my father. I didn't want-- whatever was said I put up a barrier. I just wanted to be with my father. And after eight days, we were shipped to Germany to Kaufering. We helped finish that camp, that was Kaufering IV, because there were 11 camps that were divisions of Dachau. Over there we got this number. I got 9533, and my father's number was 32. At first, there was no work there. We just carried stones from one end to the other and then took and put them back the other way. But after a while, they start taking us out to work, and I wound up working underground. It had to do something with the V2. I don't know, because it was very deep. And my work was to empty cement bags into a container. Now what this caused is that all the dust from the cement went into my eyes, and I developed a chronic infection that I have to this very day. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The paper from these bags, some of it, of course, we put around our legs and our body. Can you imagine cement paper against your body? How pleasant that is? But it kept us warm. Put it around the legs with a rope, we just tie it to keep us warm. Your father worked with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together, yes. We were together. Somehow we were lucky enough to work together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what barrack you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know or remember the name of the kapo of the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was an Algerian man, Simon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The [GERMAN]? Do you know any other people that were in charge of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was this oberscharfuhrer, Temple, but he must have been in charge of several of these camps. I'll get to him later because he came also in contact at the end of the war. We did all kind of works. We went from there to a place called the Lechfeld, which is a little village between Augsburg and Landsberg, and we laid cables. I don't even remember if we had tools to-- of course we had tools to dig. Then we worked in another ditch nearby where it was a very cold, wet November day where it was snowing wet-- wet snow-- and there were a group of Russian prisoners of war. I have to give these guys credit. I don't know how they managed to have some potatoes. I saw they had pots of potatoes. Steam was coming out, so I ran over to them like that, so they put a spoon of potatoes in my hand and I put it in my mouth. I burned my mouth, but I was eating food. I ran again to them, they gave me again. The third time, they called me a name in Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did. Why did they do that? Because they knew what it meant to be hungry. I had an experience with an American prisoner which they didn't do that because he didn't know what it meant to be hungry. He slapped me and I asked-- we're in the same place. I asked him for a piece of cabbage. He was loading cabbage into a car-- into a railcar-- without even any supervision, he was a flier.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over and I wanted a piece of cabbage. All he did is stretched out his arm and hit me so hard that my head must have moved. Yeah, well, that's an individual. That doesn't mean I'm complaining about America, but that was an individual. But these people, they were very helpful. That whole thing lasted a few months because pretty soon-- as soon as it got cold-- people started to die. Must have been typhus and they wanted to the take me and the commander to carry away the dead bodies and I kneeled down. I fell to the floor by the [GERMAN]. I don't know what came over me because those things, all they do is pull out their gun and shoot you. I said, please, I'll do any kind of work you want. Don't take me to this work. And he sent me to chop wood, gave me a little spot and I chopped wood for him. It so happens that during that time, I had a little bit more to eat. My father was inside, was laying in there in that sick bed with typhus. So I would just go in, ask him how he his. But once you're not hungry-- I wanted to sleep with my father. Never got up. Never got up from that bunker, which are these-- these were dug halfway underground and the bed was level with the ground. And above the ground, you see only a little peek like that. As a matter of fact, I have a picture that the American soldiers made when they came to this camp. Our particular camp was blown up, burned down, when they run away because the Americans were already close by, but that camp became a sick camp. A so-called hospital camp. It was so bad. We were so hungry there. We were so cold. I remember in '44 when the-- I don't know what month it was, but there was a little sun. We had no clothing, so I just had the blanket. And I went outside, barely walking, and I took the grass and sucked the grass, the juice, and spit it out. So hungry, so weak. That was a time where we knew that there is no way that we can survive. But I was with my father, and I said, I've got to help my father. It was the other way around. And at that camp, they brought people and waited till they die. Of course, at the beginning, there were things going on where you had-- at first it was the Algerian people. I don't know if they were Jewish or not. Some were Jewish, yes. Some were Jewish, that's where the kapos-- some were homosexuals where they took young boys. They called them the pupils. One I came over to me, oh, would you want to move into my-- I said, of course I want to. You'll have plenty of-- but I said, but I can't move without my father. When he heard that, another [NON-ENGLISH] in the face, he didn't need me anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: But later on, you didn't hear from because everyone was in the dumps. It was raining, snowing, and this was mud on the floor, and there was no heating or anything like that. And the latrine? Everything was frozen. Forget about washing. The lice were bigger than the people. You couldn't fight them. You couldn't fight them. And people were sleeping so close, one to the other, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was this man that came from Romania, he had oil wells, and he had this man with him, a friend or somebody to care on him. And he kept sleeping with his legs curled, like that, and the other guy said, in Hungarian, and I understood it and to this very day, I sometimes-- another supposed to be a joke but doesn't always work, he kept saying [HUNGARIAN]. Straighten out your legs. People didn't care about-- people died in the barracks next to you and you didn't even know who it was. Finally it was going from bad to worse. This would have lasted another little while-- I can't give you-- no one would have come out from there except those that are walking around because when it became so bad that it became a hospital camp, they brought in Hungarian doctors. And, of course, they were Jewish doctors. They were no better than the Algerians. They were mean. I'm not saying because they were Hungarian. These people were mean. They stole the food that even was for-- they looked healthy because they ate everything. They were doctors, they knew more than we did how to take care of ourselves. But thank God it didn't last too long. And then that was the end of April. We were transported by train from this Kaufering camp to Kaufering camp number one, which was only a few miles, but no one could walk. So we figured that they're taking us to finish us. And they took us to that camp number one because from there they had the railroad going to Dachau. The company that I worked for was a German company called [GERMAN], the builders. To my great, painful surprise when I was not too long ago in Munich, I saw a big sign, [GERMAN]. I thought they would be-- that a company like this wouldn't exist. I know Krupp exists, and others, but here I was involved with them on a daily basis, and they employed slave labor. But anyway, when we came to this camp number one, that's where we already heard the Americans guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop the tape, we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw this was blinking, so I--
